dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a transfer/interfacility/palliative care call at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I felt a pressure in my chest that made me short of breath and all sweaty earlier, but now it's about 5 out of 10.
Mr. Smith, can you respond to my questions and follow along with me?
Yes, I see you clearly.
Can you tell me your full name and confirm what day it is?
I'm Mark Smith. It's Friday, December 18.
Your airway is open and you appear to be breathing unlabored.
Patient is breathing at 16 breaths per minute. No abnormal breathing sounds detected.
Pulse is strong at 78 beats per minute. Skin is warm and dry.
Skin has normal turgor. No signs of dehydration noted.
Radial pulse is adequate with regular rhythm.
Trachea is midline. No abnormalities observed.
Your chest discomfort has been ongoing-can you explain more about when it began and what changed after taking your medications?
The discomfort has been off and on for two days. Today I took four nitroglycerin tabs, which lowered my blood pressure a bit but did not relieve the pain.
Are you allergic to any medications or drugs?
I have no known drug allergies. I take metformin/sitagliptin, aspirin daily, atorvastatin, and colchicine.
Initial blood pressure is 200 over 109. This is very high.
No signs of rashes, bruises, or skin discoloration noted.
Pupils are equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation (PERRLA). Trachea is straight without jugular vein distention.
No visible nasal bleeding, swelling, or drainage.
Clear lung sounds in all four fields bilaterally.
Chest wall is intact with no deformities or notable issues.
Abdomen is soft, without tenderness or rigidity.
Full motor function in all extremities with gross motor and sensory function (PMS x4). No evidence of swelling or injuries.
Pulse remains steady at 78 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate remains at 16 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is now 214 over 123. This is a significant increase.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
First glucose reading is 217 milligrams per deciliter.
4-lead EKG reveals sinus arrhythmia.
I am now placing a 20-gauge IV in your left arm for monitoring and possible medication administration.
Administering 324 milligrams of aspirin orally. You might notice a taste.
Adjusting you to the semi-Fowler position on the stretcher as a comfort measure.
You are now secured using five-point restraints for a safe transport.
Five-point seat belts are in place to keep you safe during transport.
Pulse is still at 77. Blood pressure is now 206 over 105.
Oxygen saturation has dropped to 99%. Glucose is unobtainable again.
12-lead EKG repeated with findings: sinus arrhythmia, first-degree AV block.
Your chest pain is still at 5/10, located in the sternum without reported radiation.
You mentioned four doses of nitroglycerin earlier. Has that changed?
No. Took them as usual. They help with the pressure slightly but not fully.
Chest pain with persistent hypertension, known CAD and a prior MI. Initiating cardiac suspected protocol at destination.
Transporting to Chippenham Hospital in the semi-Fowler's position with restraints in place.
Handing over a 50-year-old male in stable condition with known coronary artery disease and hypertension.
20-gauge IV in the left antecubital, 12-lead EKG, and 5-point restraints applied as per protocol.
No injuries reported, oxygen saturation slightly down to 99%, with CA+Ox4 documented.
Pulse and respiratory rate stable. Calibration of SpO2 confirmed.
Still 5 out of 10, no increase in discomfort, chest pressure localized.
All medications and allergies appear to be well documented per patient.
Everything looks good so far. Thank you for your care.
Transportation restraints are double-checked for safety.
Pulse remains at 77, no abnormality in rhythm or rate.
Blood pressure now is 206/105, showing slight reduction in diastolic.
SpO2 is 99% still. Room air is sufficient.
Navigation route flagged to reach Chippenham with no traffic delays reported.
Chippenham reached via phone. Bed ready. 12-lead EKG sent electronically.
No definitive temperature taken due to lack of equipment.
No need for cricothyroidal palpation; airway remains patent.
Glucometer failed twice. We will reassess on arrival if feasible.
Confirmed patient is CA+Ox4. This shows he remained alert.
The destination nurse is aware and has resources for on-arrival.
Pelvis appears intact. No positivity for instability or pain on pressure.
No complaints from back. No signs of spinal trauma during transfer.
No stiffness or abnormal neck movements noted. No jugular vein distention.
No unusual scarring or skin markings around the torso or limbs.
No guarding or rebound tenderness. Normal bowel sounds per abdomen.
No head trauma signs. Patient recalls it is December 18.
Equal and reactive pupils. No need for eye dilation or visual test.
No signs of discharge or hearing loss from ear inspection.
Dentition good. No active bleeding or sores noted around the mouth.
Normal skin coloration observed. No jaundice evident.
Skin is normal and does not feel cold or excessively warm.
Do you have a history of heart issues or significant past medical problems?
Yes. I had a heart attack a while ago and have been taking aspirin daily.
Any family medical history of similar issues? Like MI or hyperlipidemia in family?
Yes, my father had an MI at a similar age. I have primary hypertension now.
When did the chest discomfort begin today?
It first appeared around noon today.
What does it feel like? Sharp, pressure, burning, something else?
It feels like pressure, right behind my sternum. No arm or jaw pain.
How would you rate the pain level on a scale from 0 to 10?
It's a 5 right now. It can come and go but has settled for the past hour.
All points checked. Patient secured and verbal signs stable. Ready for dispatch signal.
We have the go-ahead to move. Transport route clear and destination alert.
Reporting stable vitals to dispatch. No interventions pending during transport.
Maintaining semi-Fowler to reduce chest pressure.
A few questions on checklist but everything appears good.
All restraints double-checked. Safe to proceed for hospital arrival.
Recheck: HR 77, regular. Resp 16. BP 214/105. SpO2 99%.
Navigating via safest and fastest route. Speed limit adjusted to 45 mph.
Checking for signs of deterioration or sudden change in condition.
Continuous hemodynamic monitoring initiated.
Holding a live EKG for observation against EMR readings.
Trying to get glucose again at destination if denied by equipment.
A delay at destination occurred due to decontamination requirements. Will notify hospital ahead of time.
Patient remains alert and oriented throughout the journey.
Recording final vitals before arrival as complete transport summary.
All devices and straps functional and secure.
Arrival in five minutes. ER nurse has been informed.
Transferring to ER at Chippenham's semi-Fowler position with excellent communication.
Providing EKG results, IV site status, and patient history summary to the nurse.
Vital signs stable. All interventions recorded are consistent with the field report.
We will initiate monitoring right away with a private room available.
